a: Continuous Integration means every time you push code, a service automatically merges it with everyone else's and checks that it still builds and passes tests. The goal is to catch breakages within minutes instead of weeks.
fs: CI auto-merges and verifies each change on push, so integration problems surface immediately, not at release time.
fj: Thinking CI is just running tests on your laptop. The point is an automated shared service that checks everyone's changes together, every time.

a: Continuous Delivery means once CI passes, your code is automatically packaged and made ready to ship at the press of a button. Continuous Deployment goes one step further and ships it to users automatically with no manual click.
fs: Delivery keeps every passing build release-ready for a one-click deploy; Deployment removes even that click.
fj: Mixing up the two Ds. Delivery still has a human approve the release; Deployment releases to production with zero manual steps.

a: A pipeline is the ordered list of automated steps your code runs through, such as install, build, test, and deploy. If any step fails, the pipeline stops and tells you what broke.
fs: A pipeline is a defined sequence of stages that runs on each change and halts on the first failure.
fj: Assuming later steps still run after one fails. A pipeline normally stops at the first red step, so deploy never happens on a failed test.

a: A build is the step that turns your raw source code into a runnable, packaged artifact, like a compiled program or a Docker image. The pipeline does this fresh on a clean machine so the result is repeatable.
fs: A build compiles and packages source into a deployable artifact on a clean, reproducible environment.
fj: Thinking 'it runs on my machine' equals a passing build. A clean CI machine has none of your local setup, which is exactly why builds catch missing pieces.

q: What are automated tests in CI?
a: Automated tests are checks the pipeline runs by itself to confirm your code still behaves correctly after a change. If a test fails, CI marks the change red and usually blocks it from merging or deploying.
fs: Automated tests are the gate in CI that turns a change red and blocks merge/deploy when behavior regresses.
fj: Treating a green pipeline as proof of zero bugs. Tests only catch what they cover; passing means nothing known broke, not that everything is correct.
